pattern: When you need the smallest combination of two pools, first check whether the pools overlap (a single shared item wins outright), and only fall back to combining one item from each pool -- placing the smaller digit first -- when they don't.
hints: Check whether the two arrays share any digit at all -- if they do, one shared digit alone can be your whole answer.
If nothing is shared, you'll need to use one digit from each array to build a two-digit number.

To make a two-digit number as small as possible, put the smaller of your two chosen digits in the tens place and the larger one in the ones place; and to minimize the whole result, choose the smallest digit available from each array.

edge_inputs.description: no shared digit, need a two-digit combination
a shared digit exists and beats any two-digit combo
single matching digit in both arrays
single distinct digit in each array
several shared digits, must pick the smallest one
no shared digit and the minimums must be ordered smaller-first, not by which array they came from
both arrays are full permutations of 1-9 so a shared digit is guaranteed
one array has a single small digit, the other has many larger ones

stress.description: random unique-digit subsets of varying sizes for both arrays
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n1 = rng.randint(1, 9)
    n2 = rng.randint(1, 9)
    nums1 = rng.sample(range(1, 10), n1)
    nums2 = rng.sample(range(1, 10), n2)
    return [nums1, nums2]
